questions.question: What happens during a normal TCP three-way handshake that a SYN scan deliberately avoids?
options: The initial SYN packet — SYN scans skip the SYN
The ACK that completes the connection — SYN scans send RST after receiving SYN-ACK
The FIN teardown — SYN scans skip the close sequence
DNS resolution — SYN scans use raw IP addresses only
questions.explanation: A normal handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. A SYN scan sends SYN, receives SYN-ACK (if port open), then sends RST instead of ACK. The connection is never completed. This is 'half-open' — the scanner never establishes a full session, so it often doesn't appear in the target's application logs.

questions.question: nmap reports a port as 'filtered'. What does this mean?
options: The port is open but nmap needs a password to probe it further
nmap sent a probe but received no response — likely a firewall is silently dropping packets
The port uses a non-standard protocol that nmap cannot identify
The port is closed and the OS returned an ICMP unreachable error
questions.explanation: Filtered means nmap sent a probe and got nothing back. The probe was dropped — no RST, no ICMP unreachable, nothing. This is typically a DROP rule in iptables. Compare to 'closed': a closed port actively sends back a RST (for TCP) or ICMP port unreachable (for UDP), revealing that the host is present and the port has no listener.

questions.question: You run: sudo nmap -sS -sV 10.0.0.1. The output shows: '22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 8.2p1'. How did nmap determine the exact version string?
options: It completed a full SSH login and read the server's version from the session
It sent a version-specific probe to port 22, and the SSH server returned a banner containing the version
It read the version from the target's /etc/os-release file via an HTTP request
OS fingerprinting automatically identifies all service versions
questions.explanation: SSH servers send a cleartext protocol identification banner immediately upon connection: 'SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.2p1 Ubuntu-4ubuntu0.11'. nmap's service version detection (-sV) triggers this banner and matches it against nmap-service-probes. No authentication is needed — the version is in the pre-auth banner.

questions.question: Why does a SYN scan (-sS) require root privileges while a connect scan (-sT) does not?
options: Root is needed to resolve DNS names for the target
SYN scans require raw socket access to craft packets with custom TCP flags; normal sockets only support complete connections
The kernel blocks port scanning unless the user is root
Service version detection (-sV) requires root to read /etc/services
questions.explanation: A connect scan uses the OS's normal connect() system call — available to any user. A SYN scan sends a raw IP packet with a SYN flag set, then handles the response manually (sending RST). Constructing and sending raw packets requires a raw socket (SOCK_RAW), which requires root/CAP_NET_RAW privilege on Linux.

questions.question: A host is running a service on port 8888 that is not in nmap's 1000 most common ports. You run 'sudo nmap -sS 192.168.1.50' and the port does not appear. How do you find it?
options: Run nmap with -O to enable OS fingerprinting, which finds all ports
Run nmap with -p- to scan all 65535 ports, or -p 8888 to scan that specific port
Port 8888 cannot be discovered by nmap — it requires a different tool
Run nmap with -T5 to increase speed enough to find the port
questions.explanation: By default, nmap only scans the 1000 most common ports. To scan all ports, use -p- (shorthand for -p 1-65535). This takes longer but finds services on non-standard ports. If you already know or suspect port 8888, use -p 8888 to scan just that port. Timing (-T5) and OS fingerprinting (-O) have no effect on which ports are scanned.
